one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-building survey that reframes Graph ML 'safety' as reliability + generalizability + confidentiality, each threatened by model/data/attack vectors, and catalogs the poisoning, evasion, stealing, and privacy attacks plus their defenses under one framework.
key_finding: The main takeaway is conceptual: prior Graph ML safety/trustworthiness surveys are narrow (mostly adversarial attacks), and a broader 'safety' view unifying reliability, generalizability, and confidentiality under a shared model/data/attack threat structure gives fuller coverage. The paper substantiates this with a scope-comparison table showing its coverage spans all nine cells while the seven compared surveys each cover only subsets. Concrete surveyed instances illustrate each cell, e.g., uncertainty quantification (Bayesian GCN, BGCN, GGP) and anomaly detection (DOMINANT, CoLA, CONAD) for reliability; domain adaptation (UDA-GCN, SGDA) and invariant learning (EERM, CIGA) for generalizability; differential privacy (LPGNN, GAP), graph unlearning (GNNDelete, GraphEraser), and watermarking/fingerprinting (ER Graph Watermarking, GrOVe) for confidentiality. NOTE: all of these are cited third-party works being catalogued, not results produced by this paper.
core_contribution: This is a survey paper (IEEE TKDE) that proposes a novel unifying taxonomy for 'safety' in Graph Machine Learning, not a new method or model. Its own contribution is the organizational framework, NOT the individual techniques it reviews. The framework decomposes Graph ML safety into three ASPECTS (reliability, generalizability, confidentiality) and, orthogonally, three THREAT TYPES that recur within each aspect (model threats, data threats, attack threats), yielding a 3x3 matrix. The authors populate each cell with surveyed threats and their corresponding safeguards, and argue this broader 'safety' notion is distinct from prior 'trustworthiness'/'robustness' surveys that they say overwhelmingly fixate on adversarial attacks. The paper also contributes a synthesis of open challenges and future directions per aspect.
threat_model: Because it is a survey, it catalogs many threat models rather than assuming one. The paper's own contribution is organizing them into three recurring types: (1) MODEL threats, arising from Graph ML's inherent learning mechanisms (e.g., message-passing propagating uncertainty or leaking neighbor information); (2) DATA threats, arising from graph topology and data quality (anomalous nodes/edges, out-of-distribution / distribution-shifted data, distributed/federated data); (3) ATTACK threats from adversaries. The adversarial attack threats it surveys span: data-poisoning at training time (formulated as a bilevel optimization max_G' L_atk(G',f*) s.t. f*=argmin L(G',f)), evasion at inference time, and model-stealing/IP-theft. For poisoning, it further stratifies attacker knowledge into white-box (full access to victim model, data, labels), gray-box (surrogate model, no exact params), and black-box (only input graph and model output). Model-stealing attackers train a surrogate to match a target model's accuracy/behavior via queries.
limitations: As a survey it presents no empirical evaluation, so relative effectiveness of the catalogued attacks/defenses is not measured or compared quantitatively by the authors. The taxonomy's model/data/attack partition is somewhat porous (e.g., poisoning is an 'attack threat' but also degrades 'reliability'; distribution shift appears as both a data threat and is addressed by adversarial-training defenses), and boundaries between cells are conceptual rather than crisp. Coverage is a snapshot (arXiv v1, May 2024) and inherits selection bias toward node-classification/link-prediction GNN literature. The authors themselves flag underexplored gaps: efficiency of uncertainty quantification (Monte-Carlo Bayesian GCNs are impractical at scale), anomalous-subgraph detection, OOD generalization without explicit environment splits, universal graph unlearning, and the lack of scalability/practicality evaluations for confidentiality methods.

baselines_compared: Table 1 positions this survey's scope against seven prior surveys (no accuracy numbers; entries are safety-aspect/threat-type coverage checkmarks): Li et al. [89], Liu et al. [98], Jin et al. [80], Li et al. [91], Wu et al. [167], Dai et al. [37] (trustworthy GNN survey), and Zhang et al. [203] (trustworthiness). The paper claims its own coverage spans all nine reliability/generalizability/confidentiality x model/data/attack cells while each prior survey covers only a subset.

Table 3 categorizes poisoning-attack works by target task and attacker setting (e.g., Community Detection/black-box [93]; Network Embedding/black-box [13]; Node/Graph Classification white-box [186] and gray-box Nettack [227], Metattack [228], NIPA [149], AtkSE [105]; Link Prediction white-box [11,12] and black-box Opt-Attack [148]; Trustworthiness/fairness gray-box G-FairAttack [199], FATE [81]) -- categorical, not quantitative.

Table 4 categorizes OOD-generalization methods by strategy (Adversarial Training vs Invariant Optimization vs Causality-based), task level (node/graph/size-shift), and whether domain labels are required -- categorical, not quantitative.
